questions.question: What are microservices and how do they differ from a monolith?
questions.answer:
Microservices is an architectural style where an application is structured as a collection of small, independently deployable services — each running in its own process, communicating via well-defined APIs (HTTP/REST, gRPC, messaging).

Monolith vs Microservices:

AspectMonolithMicroservices
DeploymentOne unit deployed togetherEach service deployed independently
ScalingScale the whole appScale only hot services
TechnologyOne language/frameworkPolyglot (each service chooses)
Team structureShared codebaseOne team owns one service
FailureOne bug can bring down everythingFailure is isolated to a service
ComplexitySimple to develop/debugDistributed systems complexity

In CS_PM_Service, I worked on a .NET 6 microservices setup with 8 workers and separate services. Each service had its own DB, deployed independently. Complexity was high — network calls, service discovery, distributed tracing — but each team could deploy independently without coordinating releases.
followUps: When should you NOT use microservices?
What is the strangler fig pattern for migrating from monolith?

followUpAnswers.When should you NOT use microservices?:
This is an excellent critical thinking question. Microservices carry enormous operational overhead, and the industry consensus (including from Martin Fowler and Sam Newman, who literally wrote the books on microservices) is that most systems should not start with microservices.

Do NOT use microservices when:

  • Small team (<10 engineers) — the operational overhead of managing multiple services, CI/CD pipelines, container orchestration, and distributed tracing consumes engineering capacity that should go toward features. The coordination cost exceeds the benefit.
  • Early-stage product (high uncertainty) — when your domain model is still evolving, incorrect service boundaries are expensive to fix. A monolith lets you refactor freely; splitting across network boundaries makes cross-service refactoring very painful.
  • No clear scaling asymmetry — if all parts of your system have similar load, you gain nothing from independently scaling services. The cost of the distributed system is all downside.
  • Immature DevOps/infrastructure — microservices require container orchestration (Kubernetes), service discovery, distributed tracing (OpenTelemetry), centralized logging, and independent CI/CD. Without these, you create chaos.
  • Tight data coupling between services — if services constantly need to join data across service boundaries or call each other synchronously in chains, you've split incorrectly. You now have a distributed monolith — all the complexity with none of the benefits.
// Distributed monolith anti-pattern — services are tightly coupled
// OrderService calls InventoryService calls PricingService calls UserService
// One slow service = entire chain is slow. This is worse than a monolith.
var user = await _userService.GetAsync(userId);          // network call
var pricing = await _pricingService.GetPriceAsync(sku);  // network call
var inventory = await _inventoryService.CheckAsync(sku); // network call
var order = new Order(user, pricing, inventory);         // if ANY call failed, order fails

Signs you should stay with a monolith:

  • You can't explain clearly which bounded context each service would own
  • A single feature change requires touching more than 2 services simultaneously
  • Your team doesn't have dedicated DevOps/platform engineering capacity
  • You're building an MVP or proving a business idea

Martin Fowler's recommendation: start with a "modular monolith" — a well-structured monolith with clear module boundaries, clean internal APIs, and good separation of concerns. Extract services only when you have concrete evidence that a specific module needs independent scaling or deployment.
followUpAnswers.What is the strangler fig pattern for mi:
The Strangler Fig pattern (named after the strangler fig tree that grows around a host tree and eventually replaces it) is a migration strategy for incrementally replacing a monolith with microservices — without a risky "big bang" rewrite.

How it works:

  1. Place an API Gateway or facade in front of the existing monolith — all traffic routes through it
  2. Identify a bounded context to extract (start with non-critical, well-defined boundaries)
  3. Build the new microservice in parallel with the monolith handling that function
  4. Redirect traffic for that bounded context from the monolith to the new service via the facade
  5. Once traffic is migrated and the new service is stable, delete the old monolith code for that context
  6. Repeat for the next bounded context
// Step 1: YARP gateway sits in front of everything
// All requests initially go to the monolith
{
  "ReverseProxy": {
    "Routes": {
      "legacy-catch-all": {
        "ClusterId": "monolith",
        "Match": { "Path": "/{**catch-all}" }
      }
    },
    "Clusters": {
      "monolith": {
        "Destinations": {
          "monolith-1": { "Address": "https://monolith.internal/" }
        }
      }
    }
  }
}

// Step 2: Build new OrderService microservice
// Step 3: Redirect /api/orders to the new service — monolith untouched
{
  "Routes": {
    "orders-new-service": {
      "ClusterId": "order-service",
      "Match": { "Path": "/api/orders/{**catch-all}" }
      // Higher priority than catch-all — matched first
    },
    "legacy-catch-all": {
      "ClusterId": "monolith",
      "Match": { "Path": "/{**catch-all}" }
    }
  }
}
// Monolith still handles everything EXCEPT /api/orders

Key principles for success:

  • Strangler facade first — put the gateway in front before you extract anything. Without it, you can't redirect traffic.
  • Extract leaf services first — start with services that don't call other parts of the monolith (e.g., notification service, reporting service). Avoid extracting services with heavy intra-monolith dependencies early.
  • Data migration is the hard part — each new service needs its own database. Use the database-per-service pattern and migrate data incrementally. Consider dual-write during transition: write to both old and new DB, gradually shift reads.
  • Feature flags for traffic routing — route a percentage of traffic to the new service first (canary), monitor, then gradually increase.

This pattern is significantly safer than a rewrite because the monolith continues serving users at all times — there is no cutover moment when everything must work perfectly at once.

questions.tip: Start with a monolith. Microservices have enormous operational overhead (service discovery, distributed tracing, network partitions, eventual consistency). Only split when you have clear team/scaling boundaries that justify the complexity. Most startups shouldn't use microservices.

questions.question: What is an API Gateway and what does it do?
questions.answer:
An API Gateway is a single entry point for all client requests to a microservices system. Instead of clients calling individual services directly, they call the gateway which routes to the appropriate service.

What the gateway handles:

  • Routing — /api/orders → Order Service, /api/inventory → Inventory Service
  • Authentication — validate JWT once at the gateway instead of in every service
  • Rate limiting — throttle clients before requests reach services
  • SSL termination — HTTPS at the gateway, internal HTTP between services
  • Load balancing — distribute requests across service instances
  • Request aggregation — combine responses from multiple services into one (BFF pattern)
  • Circuit breaking — stop calling a failing service

Options in .NET ecosystem:

  • YARP (Yet Another Reverse Proxy) — Microsoft's .NET-native reverse proxy, highly configurable
  • Ocelot — popular open-source API gateway for .NET
  • Azure API Management — managed cloud gateway

In CS_PM_Service we used YARP as the API gateway — it handled routing and JWT validation centrally, so individual microservices only needed authorization (not authentication).
followUps: What is the BFF (Backend for Frontend) pattern?

followUpAnswers.What is YARP?:
YARP (Yet Another Reverse Proxy) is a Microsoft-built, open-source reverse proxy library for .NET. Unlike standalone proxies (nginx, Envoy), YARP runs inside an ASP.NET Core application — you write a normal C# project that acts as a fully programmable reverse proxy. This makes it ideal for .NET-native API gateways because you can use all ASP.NET Core middleware, DI, and C# logic.

Basic YARP setup:

// Program.cs
builder.Services.AddReverseProxy()
    .LoadFromConfig(builder.Configuration.GetSection("ReverseProxy"));

app.UseRouting();
app.UseAuthentication();
app.UseAuthorization();
app.MapReverseProxy();  // adds the proxy endpoint
// appsettings.json — declarative route config (hot-reload supported)
{
  "ReverseProxy": {
    "Routes": {
      "orders-route": {
        "ClusterId": "orders-cluster",
        "Match": { "Path": "/api/orders/{**catch-all}" },
        "Transforms": [
          { "PathRemovePrefix": "/api/orders" }
        ]
      },
      "inventory-route": {
        "ClusterId": "inventory-cluster",
        "Match": { "Path": "/api/inventory/{**catch-all}" }
      }
    },
    "Clusters": {
      "orders-cluster": {
        "LoadBalancingPolicy": "RoundRobin",
        "Destinations": {
          "orders-1": { "Address": "http://order-service:8080/" },
          "orders-2": { "Address": "http://order-service-2:8080/" }
        },
        "HealthCheck": {
          "Active": { "Enabled": true, "Interval": "00:00:10", "Path": "/health" }
        }
      }
    }
  }
}

YARP key capabilities:

  • Hot-reload — route and cluster configuration changes without restart (critical for production routing changes)
  • Transforms — modify headers, paths, and query strings on requests/responses programmatically
  • Load balancing — round-robin, least requests, power-of-two-choices built in
  • Health checks — active (probe endpoint) and passive (monitor real traffic errors) health checking
  • Programmatic config — dynamically load routes from a database or service discovery at runtime
  • ASP.NET Core integration — full middleware pipeline, standard auth/auth, rate limiting via built-in ASP.NET Core middleware
// Programmatic config — load routes from your database
public class DatabaseConfigProvider : IProxyConfigProvider
{
    private readonly IRouteRepository _repo;
    public DatabaseConfigProvider(IRouteRepository repo) => _repo = repo;

    public IProxyConfig GetConfig()
    {
        var routes = _repo.GetAllRoutes();
        // Build RouteConfig and ClusterConfig from DB data
        return new InMemoryConfig(routes, clusters);
    }
}
followUpAnswers.What is the BFF (Backend for Frontend) p:
The BFF (Backend for Frontend) pattern solves the problem that different clients (mobile app, SPA web app, TV app) have very different data needs from the same microservices. Instead of one generic API gateway, you create a dedicated backend for each frontend type — each BFF aggregates, shapes, and optimizes responses for its specific client.

The problem BFF solves:

// Mobile app needs: order summary, user name, total price only
// Web SPA needs: full order detail, line items, delivery timeline, invoice PDF link
// Without BFF: clients either over-fetch (get too much data) or make multiple calls

// Without BFF — mobile must make 3 calls:
GET /api/orders/123         → full order object (too much data, slow on mobile)
GET /api/users/456          → full user profile (only needs display name)
GET /api/tracking/123       → tracking info

// With Mobile BFF — one optimized call:
GET /mobile-api/order-summary/123
// Mobile BFF internally calls all three services, combines, strips unused fields
// Returns exactly what the mobile app needs: { orderId, displayName, total, trackingStatus }

BFF implementation in .NET (YARP + aggregation):

// Mobile BFF — dedicated ASP.NET Core app
[ApiController]
[Route("mobile-api")]
public class MobileOrderController : ControllerBase
{
    private readonly IOrderServiceClient _orders;
    private readonly IUserServiceClient _users;
    private readonly ITrackingServiceClient _tracking;

    [HttpGet("order-summary/{id}")]
    public async Task<MobileOrderSummary> GetOrderSummary(int id)
    {
        // Parallel calls to multiple microservices
        var (order, user, tracking) = await (
            _orders.GetAsync(id),
            _users.GetAsync(UserId),
            _tracking.GetStatusAsync(id)
        ).WhenAll();

        // Shape for mobile: only what the mobile UI needs
        return new MobileOrderSummary
        {
            OrderId = order.Id,
            DisplayName = user.FirstName,  // not full profile
            Total = order.Total,
            Status = tracking.Status       // not full tracking timeline
        };
    }
}

// Web SPA BFF — different shaping for richer client
[HttpGet("order-detail/{id}")]
public async Task<WebOrderDetail> GetOrderDetail(int id)
{
    // Web app can handle more data and make richer UI
    return new WebOrderDetail
    {
        Order = await _orders.GetWithLineItemsAsync(id),
        User = await _users.GetFullProfileAsync(UserId),
        TrackingTimeline = await _tracking.GetFullTimelineAsync(id),
        InvoicePdfUrl = await _invoices.GetPdfUrlAsync(id)
    };
}

BFF benefits:

  • Each client gets data shaped exactly for its needs — no over-fetching or under-fetching
  • BFF teams (owned by the frontend team) can iterate independently of backend microservices
  • Authentication/session management optimized per client type (mobile uses refresh tokens, web uses cookies)
  • Downstream microservices stay clean and generic — they don't need to know about client-specific concerns

When to use BFF: When you have meaningfully different clients (mobile vs web vs 3rd party) with different data, auth, or caching requirements. Overkill if all clients need the same data.

questions.tip: API Gateway is a single point of failure — deploy it with multiple instances behind a load balancer. YARP handles hot-reload of route configuration without restart, which is crucial for zero-downtime routing changes.

questions.question: What is event-driven architecture and how does it apply to microservices?
questions.answer:
In event-driven architecture, services communicate by producing and consuming events (messages) via a message broker (RabbitMQ, Azure Service Bus, Kafka). Services are decoupled — the producer doesn't know who consumes its events.

Key concepts:

  • Event producer — publishes events when something happens ("OrderPlaced", "PaymentProcessed")
  • Message broker — stores and routes events (Azure Service Bus, RabbitMQ)
  • Event consumer — subscribes to events and processes them asynchronously
// Publisher (Order Service) — Azure Service Bus
public async Task PublishOrderPlacedAsync(Order order)
{
    var message = new ServiceBusMessage(
        JsonSerializer.SerializeToUtf8Bytes(
            new OrderPlacedEvent(order.Id, order.CustomerId, order.Total)));
    await _sender.SendMessageAsync(message);
}

// Consumer (Notification Service) — processes event async
public class OrderPlacedConsumer : BackgroundService
{
    protected override async Task ExecuteAsync(CancellationToken ct)
    {
        var processor = _serviceBusClient.CreateProcessor("order-placed-topic");
        processor.ProcessMessageAsync += async args =>
        {
            var evt = args.Message.Body.ToObjectFromJson<OrderPlacedEvent>();
            await _emailService.SendOrderConfirmationAsync(evt.CustomerId, evt.OrderId);
            await args.CompleteMessageAsync(args.Message);
        };
        await processor.StartProcessingAsync(ct);
    }
}

followUpAnswers.What is eventual consistency?:
Eventual consistency is a consistency model used in distributed systems where, given no new updates, all replicas of data will eventually converge to the same value — but at any given moment in time, different nodes or services may have stale data.

In microservices, when services have their own databases and communicate asynchronously via events, strong consistency (like a database transaction) is impossible across service boundaries. Eventual consistency is the trade-off you accept for decoupling and scalability.

Concrete example:

// Time 0: User places order
// OrderService: saves Order{Status=Placed} to orders.db
// OrderService: publishes "OrderPlaced" event to Service Bus

// Time 0-50ms: event is in transit
// At this moment:
//   OrderService orders.db: Status = Placed
//   InventoryService inventory.db: Stock NOT yet decremented (event not processed)
//   System is INCONSISTENT

// Time 50ms: InventoryService receives event
// InventoryService: decrements stock in inventory.db
// InventoryService: publishes "InventoryReserved" event

// Time 100ms: System is now eventually consistent
//   OrderService: Status = Placed
//   InventoryService: Stock decremented
//   Both databases reflect the same reality

// What if user queries inventory between 0ms and 50ms?
public async Task<int> GetAvailableStockAsync(string sku)
{
    // Returns stale count — hasn't been decremented yet
    // This is the nature of eventual consistency
    return await _db.Inventory.Where(i => i.Sku == sku).Select(i => i.Quantity).FirstAsync();
}

Handling eventual consistency in practice:

  • Design the UI for it — show "Your order is being processed" instead of immediate confirmation. Use optimistic UI that assumes success.
  • Idempotent consumers — events may be delivered more than once ("at-least-once" delivery). Make processing idempotent: processing the same event twice should produce the same result.
  • Saga pattern — for multi-step workflows, use a saga to coordinate compensating transactions if a step fails (e.g., if payment fails after inventory was reserved, publish a "ReleaseInventory" command).
// Idempotent consumer — safe to process same event multiple times
public async Task HandleOrderPlacedAsync(OrderPlacedEvent evt)
{
    // Check if we already processed this event
    var alreadyProcessed = await _db.ProcessedEvents
        .AnyAsync(e => e.EventId == evt.EventId);

    if (alreadyProcessed)
    {
        _logger.LogInformation("Event {Id} already processed — skipping", evt.EventId);
        return;  // idempotent: no side effects on duplicate
    }

    await _inventoryDb.DecrementStockAsync(evt.Sku, evt.Quantity);
    await _db.ProcessedEvents.AddAsync(new ProcessedEvent { EventId = evt.EventId });
    await _db.SaveChangesAsync();
}
followUpAnswers.What is the Outbox pattern?:
The Outbox pattern solves the dual-write problem: in event-driven microservices, you need to atomically save to the database AND publish an event. These are two separate systems, so they cannot be in the same ACID transaction. If you save to the DB and then crash before publishing the event, the event is permanently lost — causing data inconsistency across services.

The problem:

// DANGEROUS: Two-phase commit across different systems — not atomic!
public async Task PlaceOrderAsync(Cart cart)
{
    var order = Order.Create(cart);
    await _orderDb.SaveAsync(order);          // Step 1: saved to DB

    // App crashes HERE — event never published!
    // OrderService DB has the order but InventoryService never decrements stock
    // Inventory is now permanently inconsistent

    await _serviceBus.PublishAsync(new OrderPlacedEvent(order)); // Step 2: never reached
}

The Outbox pattern solution:

// SAFE: Write event to Outbox table in the SAME DB transaction as business data
public async Task PlaceOrderAsync(Cart cart)
{
    var order = Order.Create(cart);
    var outboxMessage = new OutboxMessage
    {
        Id = Guid.NewGuid(),
        EventType = nameof(OrderPlacedEvent),
        Payload = JsonSerializer.Serialize(new OrderPlacedEvent(order.Id, order.Total)),
        CreatedAt = DateTime.UtcNow,
        ProcessedAt = null  // null = not yet published
    };

    // Single atomic transaction — either both save or neither does
    await using var tx = await _db.Database.BeginTransactionAsync();
    _db.Orders.Add(order);
    _db.OutboxMessages.Add(outboxMessage);
    await _db.SaveChangesAsync();
    await tx.CommitAsync();

    // If we crash here, the Outbox message is still in the DB and will be published by the relay
}

// Outbox Relay — background service that polls the Outbox table and publishes events
public class OutboxRelayService : BackgroundService
{
    protected override async Task ExecuteAsync(CancellationToken ct)
    {
        while (!ct.IsCancellationRequested)
        {
            var unpublished = await _db.OutboxMessages
                .Where(m => m.ProcessedAt == null)
                .OrderBy(m => m.CreatedAt)
                .Take(50)
                .ToListAsync(ct);

            foreach (var message in unpublished)
            {
                try
                {
                    await _serviceBus.PublishAsync(message.EventType, message.Payload, ct);

                    message.ProcessedAt = DateTime.UtcNow;
                    await _db.SaveChangesAsync(ct);
                }
                catch (Exception ex)
                {
                    _logger.LogError(ex, "Failed to publish outbox message {Id}", message.Id);
                    // Will retry on next poll
                }
            }

            await Task.Delay(TimeSpan.FromSeconds(5), ct);
        }
    }
}

Outbox guarantees:

  • At-least-once delivery — the event will always eventually be published (even if the relay has to retry)
  • No lost events — the outbox message persists in the DB until confirmed published
  • Consumer must be idempotent — because at-least-once means duplicate delivery is possible

Production libraries like MassTransit (with the UseEntityFrameworkOutbox() extension) and NServiceBus implement the Outbox pattern for you with full transactional guarantees.

questions.tip: Outbox pattern — solves the dual-write problem: saving to DB and publishing an event are not atomic. If you save and crash before publishing, the event is lost. Solution: write events to an Outbox DB table in the same transaction, then a background job publishes them reliably.

questions.question: What is the Circuit Breaker pattern and why is it important in microservices?
questions.answer:
The Circuit Breaker pattern prevents cascading failures in distributed systems. When a downstream service starts failing, the circuit breaker opens (stops sending requests) and returns a fallback response immediately — without waiting for timeouts that would cascade through the system.

Three states:

  • Closed — normal operation, requests flow through
  • Open — service is failing, requests short-circuit immediately (no calls made)
  • Half-Open — trial state: allow a few requests through to test if service recovered

Implementation with Polly:

// Program.cs
builder.Services.AddHttpClient<IInventoryService, InventoryService>()
    .AddStandardResilienceHandler(options =>
    {
        // Retry: 3 attempts with exponential backoff
        options.Retry.MaxRetryAttempts = 3;
        options.Retry.Delay = TimeSpan.FromMilliseconds(200);
        options.Retry.BackoffType = DelayBackoffType.Exponential;

        // Circuit Breaker
        options.CircuitBreaker.FailureRatio = 0.5;    // open if 50% fail
        options.CircuitBreaker.SamplingDuration = TimeSpan.FromSeconds(10);
        options.CircuitBreaker.BreakDuration = TimeSpan.FromSeconds(30);
    });

In CS_PM_Service, when the payment gateway was down, the circuit breaker opened immediately — preventing the entire order service from hanging with 30-second timeouts on every request.

followUpAnswers.What is Polly?:
Polly is the standard resilience and transient-fault-handling library for .NET. It provides a fluent API to define resilience strategies — policies that wrap your code and handle failures gracefully. In .NET 8, Polly v8 is the foundation of Microsoft.Extensions.Http.Resilience (the AddStandardResilienceHandler() API).

Polly v8 core strategies:

using Polly;
using Polly.Retry;
using Polly.CircuitBreaker;
using Polly.Timeout;
using Polly.Fallback;

// 1. RETRY — retry transient failures
var retryPipeline = new ResiliencePipelineBuilder()
    .AddRetry(new RetryStrategyOptions
    {
        MaxRetryAttempts = 3,
        Delay = TimeSpan.FromMilliseconds(200),
        BackoffType = DelayBackoffType.Exponential,  // 200ms, 400ms, 800ms
        UseJitter = true,  // add randomness to avoid thundering herd
        ShouldHandle = new PredicateBuilder()
            .Handle<HttpRequestException>()
            .Handle<TaskCanceledException>(),
        OnRetry = args =>
        {
            Console.WriteLine($"Retry {args.AttemptNumber} after {args.RetryDelay}");
            return ValueTask.CompletedTask;
        }
    })
    .Build();

// 2. CIRCUIT BREAKER
var cbPipeline = new ResiliencePipelineBuilder()
    .AddCircuitBreaker(new CircuitBreakerStrategyOptions
    {
        FailureRatio = 0.5,                           // open when 50% fail
        SamplingDuration = TimeSpan.FromSeconds(10),  // measured over 10s window
        MinimumThroughput = 10,                        // need at least 10 requests
        BreakDuration = TimeSpan.FromSeconds(30),      // stay open for 30s
        ShouldHandle = new PredicateBuilder().Handle<HttpRequestException>()
    })
    .Build();

// 3. TIMEOUT — fail fast if too slow
var timeoutPipeline = new ResiliencePipelineBuilder()
    .AddTimeout(TimeSpan.FromSeconds(3))
    .Build();

// 4. FALLBACK — return a default when all else fails
var fallbackPipeline = new ResiliencePipelineBuilder<string>()
    .AddFallback(new FallbackStrategyOptions<string>
    {
        FallbackAction = _ =>
            ValueTask.FromResult(Outcome.FromResult("Service unavailable"))
    })
    .Build();

// 5. COMBINED PIPELINE (recommended order: outermost to innermost)
var fullPipeline = new ResiliencePipelineBuilder<HttpResponseMessage>()
    .AddFallback(...)      // 1st (outermost): catch everything that falls through
    .AddCircuitBreaker(...)  // 2nd: stop calling failing services
    .AddRetry(...)           // 3rd: retry transient errors
    .AddTimeout(...)         // 4th (innermost): fail individual attempts quickly
    .Build();

With HttpClient in ASP.NET Core (most common usage):

// Simplest: AddStandardResilienceHandler() — configures retry + CB + timeout with sensible defaults
builder.Services.AddHttpClient<IOrderService, OrderService>()
    .AddStandardResilienceHandler();

// Custom:
builder.Services.AddHttpClient<IPaymentService, PaymentService>()
    .AddResilienceHandler("payment-pipeline", builder =>
    {
        builder
            .AddFallback(new HttpFallbackStrategyOptions { ... })
            .AddCircuitBreaker(new HttpCircuitBreakerStrategyOptions { BreakDuration = TimeSpan.FromSeconds(15) })
            .AddRetry(new HttpRetryStrategyOptions { MaxRetryAttempts = 2 });
    });
followUpAnswers.What is bulkhead isolation?:
Bulkhead isolation is a resilience pattern inspired by ship hull compartments (bulkheads) — if one compartment floods, watertight walls prevent the water from flooding the whole ship. Applied to software: isolate failures in one component so they cannot consume all resources and bring down other components.

In microservices, the common failure mode is thread pool / connection pool exhaustion: if Service A is slow, callers pile up waiting threads. Eventually all threads are blocked waiting for Service A, and other services (B, C) that are perfectly healthy can't get threads either — the whole app freezes.

How bulkhead isolation prevents this:

// Without bulkhead: all HTTP calls share the same thread pool
// ServiceA goes slow → 500 threads pile up → ServiceB and ServiceC starve

// WITH bulkhead: each service gets its own semaphore (resource limit)
var bulkheadForPaymentService = new ResiliencePipelineBuilder()
    .AddConcurrencyLimiter(new ConcurrencyLimiterStrategyOptions
    {
        // Max 10 concurrent calls to Payment Service
        PermittedConcurrencyLimitPerEndpoint = 10,
        // Queue up to 20 more waiting; reject beyond that immediately
        QueuedTasksLimit = 20,
        // Queued calls wait at most 2 seconds before rejection
        OnRejected = args =>
        {
            Console.WriteLine("Payment service bulkhead full — rejecting");
            return ValueTask.CompletedTask;
        }
    })
    .Build();

// Payment service can now only consume 10 threads max
// Even if it hangs, Inventory and Shipping services are unaffected

HttpClient bulkhead with Polly + ASP.NET Core:

builder.Services.AddHttpClient<IPaymentService, PaymentService>()
    .AddResilienceHandler("payment", pipeline =>
    {
        pipeline
            .AddConcurrencyLimiter(10)   // max 10 concurrent HTTP calls
            .AddCircuitBreaker(new HttpCircuitBreakerStrategyOptions())
            .AddRetry(new HttpRetryStrategyOptions { MaxRetryAttempts = 2 })
            .AddTimeout(TimeSpan.FromSeconds(5));
    });

// Inventory service gets its OWN separate limits
builder.Services.AddHttpClient<IInventoryService, InventoryService>()
    .AddResilienceHandler("inventory", pipeline =>
    {
        pipeline
            .AddConcurrencyLimiter(20)   // inventory can have more concurrency
            .AddCircuitBreaker(new HttpCircuitBreakerStrategyOptions());
    });

Thread pool bulkhead via separate channels (advanced):

  • Use separate Channel<T> or SemaphoreSlim instances per downstream dependency
  • Kubernetes: run critical services as separate deployments so one crashing pod can't kill co-located services
  • Database bulkhead: separate connection pools per service (don't let reporting queries starve transactional queries)

Bulkhead is complementary to Circuit Breaker: Circuit Breaker stops calls once a service is failing. Bulkhead limits concurrent calls even during normal operation, preventing a slow service from saturating your thread pool before the circuit has a chance to open.

questions.tip: Polly is the standard resilience library for .NET. In .NET 8, use AddStandardResilienceHandler() (from Microsoft.Extensions.Http.Resilience) which configures retry + circuit breaker + timeout with sensible defaults. Don't roll your own retry logic.

questions.question: What is Clean Architecture and how does it relate to microservices?
questions.answer:
Clean Architecture (Robert C. Martin) organizes code into concentric layers where inner layers don't know about outer layers:

  • Domain (innermost) — entities, value objects, domain events. Pure C# — no external dependencies.
  • Application — use cases (commands/queries), interfaces (IRepository), DTOs. Depends only on Domain.
  • Infrastructure — EF Core, HTTP clients, email senders. Implements Application interfaces.
  • Presentation (outermost) — controllers, Razor views. Depends on Application.
MyApp.Domain/          # Entities, Value Objects, Domain Events
  └── Entities/
  └── ValueObjects/
MyApp.Application/     # Use Cases, Interfaces, DTOs
  └── Portfolios/
      └── Commands/
      └── Queries/
  └── Interfaces/
MyApp.Infrastructure/  # EF Core, SMTP, HTTP, etc.
  └── Persistence/
  └── Email/
MyApp.Api/             # Controllers, Middleware, DI wiring

Dependency Rule: Source code dependencies always point inward. Domain knows nothing about EF Core or HTTP. This makes the domain testable in isolation and infrastructure replaceable.

Clean Architecture works at any scale — each microservice can be a Clean Architecture app internally.
followUps: What is the Dependency Inversion Principle in Clean Architecture?
Clean Architecture vs Vertical Slice Architecture?

followUpAnswers.What is the Dependency Inversion Princip:
DIP is the architectural foundation that makes Clean Architecture's layer isolation possible. Without DIP, the inner layers (Domain, Application) would have to reference concrete implementations in the outer layers (Infrastructure) — which would collapse the layer boundaries.

How DIP enables Clean Architecture's Dependency Rule:

// In MyApp.Application (inner layer) — defines the ABSTRACTION
// Application layer owns the interface — it dictates what it needs
public interface IOrderRepository
{
    Task<Order?> FindAsync(Guid id, CancellationToken ct);
    Task AddAsync(Order order, CancellationToken ct);
    Task SaveChangesAsync(CancellationToken ct);
}

// In MyApp.Application — use case depends ONLY on the abstraction
public class PlaceOrderCommandHandler : IRequestHandler<PlaceOrderCommand, Guid>
{
    private readonly IOrderRepository _orders;  // abstraction — not EF Core!
    private readonly IEventPublisher _events;   // abstraction — not Service Bus!

    public PlaceOrderCommandHandler(IOrderRepository orders, IEventPublisher events)
    {
        _orders = orders;
        _events = events;
    }

    public async Task<Guid> Handle(PlaceOrderCommand cmd, CancellationToken ct)
    {
        var order = Order.Create(cmd.CustomerId, cmd.Items);
        await _orders.AddAsync(order, ct);
        await _orders.SaveChangesAsync(ct);
        await _events.PublishAsync(new OrderPlacedEvent(order.Id), ct);
        return order.Id;
    }
    // Zero references to EF Core, Service Bus, or any infrastructure — pure business logic
}

// In MyApp.Infrastructure (outer layer) — concrete implementation
// Infrastructure depends on Application (inward), not the other way
public class EfOrderRepository : IOrderRepository
{
    private readonly AppDbContext _db;
    public EfOrderRepository(AppDbContext db) => _db = db;

    public async Task<Order?> FindAsync(Guid id, CancellationToken ct)
        => await _db.Orders.FindAsync([id], ct);

    public async Task AddAsync(Order order, CancellationToken ct)
        => await _db.Orders.AddAsync(order, ct);

    public async Task SaveChangesAsync(CancellationToken ct)
        => await _db.SaveChangesAsync(ct);
}

// In MyApp.Api/Program.cs — DI wiring at the composition root
builder.Services.AddScoped<IOrderRepository, EfOrderRepository>();
builder.Services.AddScoped<IEventPublisher, ServiceBusEventPublisher>();

The flow of dependencies:

  • Domain: no dependencies (pure C#)
  • Application: depends on Domain interfaces only
  • Infrastructure: depends on Application (to implement its interfaces) and Domain (for entities)
  • Api: depends on Application (to call use cases), wires everything via DI

Why this matters in tests:

// Application layer is 100% testable without EF Core, Service Bus, or any infrastructure
[Test]
public async Task PlaceOrder_ShouldPublishEvent()
{
    var fakeRepo = new FakeOrderRepository();  // simple in-memory fake
    var fakeEvents = new FakeEventPublisher();
    var handler = new PlaceOrderCommandHandler(fakeRepo, fakeEvents);

    await handler.Handle(new PlaceOrderCommand(...), CancellationToken.None);

    Assert.That(fakeEvents.Published, Has.One.InstanceOf<OrderPlacedEvent>());
    // No DB, no network, no docker — just pure C# logic tested
}
followUpAnswers.Clean Architecture vs Vertical Slice Arc:
These are two competing approaches to organizing code within a service, and both are valid — they optimize for different things. Understanding the trade-offs is what interviewers look for.

Clean Architecture organizes by technical layer:

MyApp/
├── Domain/
│   ├── Entities/Order.cs
│   ├── Entities/Customer.cs
│   └── ValueObjects/Money.cs
├── Application/
│   ├── Orders/Commands/PlaceOrderCommand.cs
│   ├── Orders/Commands/PlaceOrderCommandHandler.cs
│   ├── Orders/Queries/GetOrderQuery.cs
│   ├── Customers/Commands/RegisterCustomerCommand.cs
│   └── Interfaces/IOrderRepository.cs
├── Infrastructure/
│   ├── Persistence/EfOrderRepository.cs
│   └── Email/SendGridEmailSender.cs
└── Api/
    ├── Controllers/OrdersController.cs
    └── Controllers/CustomersController.cs

Vertical Slice Architecture organizes by feature/use case:

MyApp/
├── Features/
│   ├── PlaceOrder/
│   │   ├── PlaceOrderCommand.cs
│   │   ├── PlaceOrderHandler.cs     # contains EF Core, business logic, all in one slice
│   │   ├── PlaceOrderValidator.cs
│   │   └── PlaceOrderController.cs
│   ├── GetOrder/
│   │   ├── GetOrderQuery.cs
│   │   ├── GetOrderHandler.cs       # may use Dapper directly for simple reads
│   │   └── GetOrderResponse.cs
│   └── RegisterCustomer/
│       ├── RegisterCustomerCommand.cs
│       ├── RegisterCustomerHandler.cs
│       └── RegisterCustomerController.cs
└── Shared/
    ├── DbContext.cs
    └── MediatR pipeline behaviors

Comparison:

AspectClean ArchitectureVertical Slice
Organized byTechnical layerFeature/use case
Adding a featureChange files in 4+ foldersAdd one new feature folder
Team scalingLayer conflicts (multiple devs in Domain/)Each team owns their feature folder
Code reuseStrong — shared domain modelLess enforced — duplication tolerated
Domain complexityBetter for rich domain modelsBetter for CRUD/simple use cases
OnboardingSteeper learning curve"Find the feature, change the feature"
Testing approachUnit test domain/application in isolationIntegration test slices end-to-end

When to choose which:

  • Clean Architecture: complex domain logic, multiple aggregates interacting, team needs strong separation between business rules and infrastructure, domain is the primary concern
  • Vertical Slice: CRUD-heavy service, rapid feature development, multiple teams each owning different features, slices are truly independent (order features don't share much with customer features)

Hybrid approach (what many production teams use): Vertical Slice for feature organization at the top level, but still keep a shared Domain layer for the core business entities and invariants that truly are shared across slices.

questions.tip: Vertical Slice Architecture is an alternative — organize by feature (Portfolio creation, Portfolio listing) rather than technical layers. Each feature has its own command, handler, validator, and query in one folder. Scales better with teams than the horizontal Clean Architecture layers.
